coredata.dc:title: Thioacetamide-derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots for “green” quantum dot solar cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Riaz, Shahina
Park, Soo-Jin
coredata.dc:description: To make quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) competitive, a power conversion efficiency (PCE) comparable to other developing solar cells is required. Significant attention has been paid to undoped or nitrogen (N) doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) for use as sensitizers or light harvesters in solar cells. However, to our knowledge, the nitrogen and sulfur (S) co-doped CQDs (N, S-CQDs) have never been used as the absorbing layer alone. In the present work, we synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) from a single precursor thioacetamide (TA), via a hydrothermal method. The as-prepared N, S-CQDs were employed as a “green” photoactive layer in TiO2 film, as photoanode. The solar cell delivered an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.43 V, short-circuit current (Jsc) of 0.61 mA/cm−2, fill factor (FF) of 52 %, and PCE of 1.36%; highest among all the carbon-based QDSCs. Moreover, Device-NS showed more stability over 48 h compared to Device-C and Device-N. Undeniably, the achieved PCE is not satisfactory; however, the upgraded device fabrication and structural design may improve PCE and current densities while sustaining the high open-circuit voltage. This study demonstrated the potential application of N, S-CQDs for low-cost, “green” quantum dot solar cell applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eco-friendly solar cell
Carbon-based green sensitizers
Power conversion efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S-CQDs, N-CQDs, and CQDs

This work was also supported by Nano-Convergence Foundation(www.nanotech2020.org) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT(MSIT, Korea)& the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy(MOTIE, Korea). [Project Name: Development of high-efficiency activated carbon filter for removing indoor harmful elements (VOCs, radon, bacteria, etc.)]

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Formation of NS-CQDs and (b) solar cell device assembly.
(a) UV–Visible, (b) Tauc plots (c) PL spectra, and (d) Raman spectra of QDs.
(a) XPS survey scan, (b) C1s, (c) O1s. (d) N1s, (e) S2p, and (f) elemental analysis of N, S-CQDs.
(a) full valence band spectrum of N, S-CQDs and (b) Energy band structure.
(a)energy levels diagram (b)J-V curves (c) Transient photocurrentdensity, and (d) IPCEspectra of all the devices.
(a-c) EIS spectra of all the devices at various biases (d) appropriate circuit for EIS measurements.

Evaluation of stability of different photovoltaic parameters with respect to time (a) Current density (Jsc), (b) Open circuit voltage (Voc), (c) Fill factor (FF), and (d) Power conversion efficiency (PCE).
J-V curves of Device-NS (b) transient photocurrent density measurements of Device-NS at different light intensities.
Photovoltaic parameters of all the devices under 100 mW/cm2 light intensity.
Photovoltaic parameters of Device-NS at different light intensities.
A comparison of results obtained in this study with the already published literature.

head.ce:title: Thioacetamide-derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots for “green” quantum dot solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Inha University, 100 Inharo, Incheon 22212, Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Inha University, 100 Inharo, Incheon 22212, Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) were synthesized from a single precursor thioacetamide (TA), via a hydrothermal method. The method is facile and N, S-CQDs are synthesized with no need for surface ligands, high temperature, or other intricate chemical techniques. The as-prepared N, S-CQDs were employed as a “green” photoactive layer in TiO2 film, as photoanode, using a Pt counter electrode and I- 3/I- electrolyte. The solar cell delivered a PCE of 1.36%; highest among all the carbon-based QDSCs.

To make quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) competitive, a power conversion efficiency (PCE) comparable to other developing solar cells is required. Significant attention has been paid to undoped or nitrogen (N) doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) for use as sensitizers or light harvesters in solar cells. However, to our knowledge, the nitrogen and sulfur (S) co-doped CQDs (N, S-CQDs) have never been used as the absorbing layer alone. In the present work, we synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) from a single precursor thioacetamide (TA), via a hydrothermal method. The as-prepared N, S-CQDs were employed as a “green” photoactive layer in TiO2 film, as photoanode. The solar cell delivered an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.43 V, short-circuit current (Jsc) of 0.61 mA/cm−2, fill factor (FF) of 52 %, and PCE of 1.36%; highest among all the carbon-based QDSCs. Moreover, Device-NS showed more stability over 48 h compared to Device-C and Device-N. Undeniably, the achieved PCE is not satisfactory; however, the upgraded device fabrication and structural design may improve PCE and current densities while sustaining the high open-circuit voltage. This study demonstrated the potential application of N, S-CQDs for low-cost, “green” quantum dot solar cell applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental safety and the demand for “green” energy are humankind's primary concerns [1,2]. Due to the increased energy consumption, exploring highly efficient, environment friendly, and economical solar cells is undeniably a promising choice [3,4]. Quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs) show promise due to their tunable bandgap and higher absorption range [5–7]. Fabrication of highly efficient QDs is a demanding task for optoelectronic devices. QDs exhibit unique characteristics such as tunable band gap, wide absorption range, high resistivity against the photo-bleaching behavior, and higher extinction coefficient [8,9]. The optimal architectural design of QDs as a light harvester, in sensitized solar cells, has attracted research efforts [10]. In general, metal (Cd or Pb) sulfides QDs, with different architectural designs, are used as sensitizers in TiO2-based solar cells with maximum power conversion efficiency (PCE) as high as 12.75% [11–13]. But, the toxic nature and charge recombination of these metal sulfides QDs are the major disadvantages for the fabrication of QD sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) [10,14]. To overcome these issues, a preferred solution is to develop green QDs with In or Cu-based materials [15–17]. But these QDs are passivated with long-chain ligands. These ligands must be recovered before designing the solar cell device. Apart from this, the synthesis of these inorganic QDs. with desirable size and shape, involves complicated synthesis procedures [18–20]. Additionally, the corrosion of these inorganic QDs in the presence of I−/I electrolyte results in decreased PCEs of solar cells due to lower stability. Therefore, eco-friendly “green” materials are required and welcomed in the production of photovoltaic devices [21–23].

Owing to the exceptional electronic and optical properties, low toxicity, water solubility, and chemical resistivity, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are considered as efficient light-absorbing materials for photovoltaic applications [24–30]. Immense efforts have been made to introduce CQDs into photoelectric devices. The size and surface groups of CQDs depend on the synthesis method. Based on the synthesis method, CQDs have different nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur-containing functional groups on the surface, which affects their properties [31]. CQDs are widely used as sensitizers or co-sensitizers for QDSCs [32–34]. Wang et al., [34] prepared nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) and constructed the N-doped solar cell device, with PCE of 0.79 %. Carolan et al., [35] prepared N-CQDs by atmospheric pressure and plasma treatment and employed them as a photoactive layer for the fabrication of solar cells, achieving a maximum PCE of 0.8%. Mirtchev et al., [36] used CQDs as sensitizers for the fabrication of QDSCs and achieved 0.13% PCE. Zhang et al., [37] prepared N-CQDs for QDSCs with 0.13% PCE. Mistry et al., [38] had also prepared N-CQDs from sugar cane and employed them as a light harvester in TiO2-based solar cells and achieved maximum PCE of 1.20%. Majumder and Mondal, [39] reported the fabrication of S, N-co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N-GQDs), decorated ZnO nanorods for quantum dot sensitized solar cells, and achieved the PCE of 0.27 %. So far, the CQDs sensitized solar cells exhibit very low PCE which might be due to the wider gaps between conduction bands (CB) of TiO2 and CQDs which results in charge recombination leading to lower short-circuit current density (Jsc).

Based on previous literature, heteroatom doping is a way to minimize the gap between CB of TiO2 and CB of CQDs. Heteroatoms such as N and sulfur (S) are considered as suitable candidates to make intermediary electronic energy levels in the bandgap which are excited by visible light which gives sufficient photocarriers into the CB of TiO2. Jin et al., [40] reported that charge transfer between amine groups and CQDs caused a red-shift in the photoluminescence of CQDs, demonstrated a high fluorescent quantum yield and excitation-dependent emission of N an S co-doped CQDs. These results suggest that heteroatom doping plays a key role in tuning the optical properties of CQDs. The electron-rich S-atom will enhance the electron-donating ability of CQDs [41]. Considering this, we prepared N and S co-doped CQDs (N, S-CQDs) from a single precursor thioacetamide (TA) as carbon (C), N, and S source, via hydrothermal method. The method is facile, economical, and environmentally benign. The as-prepared N, S-CQDs were employed as a green sensitizer/photoactive layer for the fabrication of TiO2-based QD solar cells (TQDSCs), without the need for additional sensitizing material. For comparison, CQDs and N-CQDs sensitized TQDSCs were also prepared. As for as we know, this is the first study in which N, S-CQDs are employed as light harvesters for the fabrication of TQDSCs and achieved 1.36% of PCE; the highest amongst the carbon-based quantum dot sensitized solar cells (C-QDSCs). We believe that the proposed fabrication of TQDSCs, using green N, S-CQDs from a single precursor, is favorable and convenient strategy for light harvesting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S-CQDs, N-CQDs, and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: N, S-CQDs were prepared by following the method reported in [42]. Briefly, 0.5 g of thioacetamide was added to 50 mL of deionized water and stirred to make a clear solution. Thereafter, the mixture was moved to a 100 mL autoclave and heated in an oven, at 200 °C, for 5 h. Finally, the solution was filtered and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm, for 30 min, to remove the larger particles. The yellow supernatant was freeze-dried and saved at 4 °C for further use (Fig. 1 a). N-CQDs were fabricated using melamine and citric acid (CA) by hydrothermal method reported by Liu et al., [43] following the same protocol as above. CQDs were prepared by following the same protocol as above without the addition of melamine.

The photoanode was prepared according to the previous report [44]. Cleaned FTO substrate was immersed in 40 mM TiCl4 aqueous solution at 80 °C, for 20 min, followed by washing with water and anhydrous ethanol. Thereafter, a 20 nm-sized TiO2 paste was placed by the doctor blade method and the substrate was dried at 130 °C, for 10 min, followed by coating of 200 nm-sized TiO2 paste. After this, the substrate was sintered in air at 400° C, for 20 min. After cooling to 80 °C, the TiO2 film was dipped in the 50 mg/ml N, S-CQDs aqueous solution for 24 h in the dark. The N, S-CQDs loading was calculated to be ∼1 mg cm−2 (N, S-CQD/TiO2). Finally, the N, S-CQDs sensitized TiO2 film was washed with anhydrous ethanol and dried in nitrogen steam. The device was fabricated by injecting the I−/I electrolyte solution between the TiO2 photoanode and the Pt counter electrode (CE). The electrodes were sealed by melting a thin transparent film of polymer gasket at 70 °C. The device was named Device-NS. Device-N and Device-C were prepared by following the above-mentioned procedure and N, S-CQDs were replaced with N-CQDs and CQDs, respectively.

The N, S-CQDs were prepared by a simple and economical hydrothermal method using thioacetamide as a single precursor for carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur. Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of as-prepared N, S-CQDs in different magnifications. In the synthesis of QDs, it is crucial to optimize the variables involved in the synthetic protocol such as reaction time, temperature, solvent, and concentration of precursors, because these parameters significantly affect the properties of QDs (i.e., morphology, size of QDs, and other spectroscopic characteristics) [45]. From the literature, we determined that 200 °C and 5 h reaction time are the optimal reaction conditions for the synthesis of N, S-CQDs [42]. As seen in Fig. 2a and b, the QDs exhibit a spherical shape and are uniform in size ranging from 2 to 7.5 nm. The calculated average diameter of N, S-CQDs is 4.3 nm (inset Fig. 1a). The HR-TEM images (Fig. 2c) of the N, S-CQDs indicate a 0.24 nm distance between the fringes ascribed to (1120) the lattice fringe of graphene, which corresponds well with the already reported literature [41,46–48]. Fig. 3 presents the FTIR spectra of as-prepared N, S-CQDs, N-CQDs, and CQDs. The FTIR spectra consist of CS, CN, CN, CO, CHx, and OH bands at 1015, 1395, 1636, 1707, 2835–2927, and 3112–3579 cm−1, respectively. The presence of CS, CN, and CN bands confirms the presence of S and N atoms on the carbon conjugated system.

Fig. 4 a displays the UV-Vis absorption spectra of CQDs, N-CQDs, and N, S-CQDs. As can be seen from the spectrum, the absorption of CQDs exhibited a redshift when their graphite lattice is doped with N or co-doped with N and S; N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs exhibit broad peaks centered at ∼361 nm, and ∼336 and ∼440 nm which are ascribed to the aromatic carbon sp2 domains (π-π*/σ-π*) and the surface-mediated absorptions such as n-π* electronic transitions of the CO, CN, and –COR functional groups present on the N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs, respectively [42,49–52]. As seen in the UV-Vis spectra, all the prepared QDs display absorption tails ranging into the visible region, making them suitable candidates as solar absorber agents. The band gap of all the prepared QDs samples was calculated by producing the Tauc plots as shown in Fig. 4b.

Fitting the linear part of the spectra and extrapolation of (αhν)1/2 vs energy (eV) plot gives the band gap values 3.51, 3.44, and 3.31 eV for CQDs, N-CQDs, and N, S-CQDs, respectively. This result demonstrates a band gap decrease with the N and S co-doping, which corresponds well with the already published literature [53]. This quantum confinement converts the QDs to non-zero band gap semiconductors, which have PL upon excitation [54]. Fig. 4c shows the PL spectra for all the prepared QDs samples. The emission was observed in the visible region. CQDs and N-CQDs exhibit PL peaks at 500 and 434 nm, respectively. However, N, S-CQDs exhibit two characteristic PL peaks at 432 and 612 nm, and the PL position showed a red-shift to longer wavelengths.

Raman spectra of prepared CQDs, N-CQDs, and N, S-CQDs is presented in Fig. 4d. As seen in Figure, all the prepared samples exhibit two characteristic peaks corresponding to the D and G bands. The dispersed band (D-band) is associated with the vibrational modes of the dangling bond. Whereas, the G-band arises due to the scattered stretching vibrations that correspond to the sp2 carbon [55]. CQDs displayed two peaks at 1452 (D-band) and 1517 cm−1 (G-band), respectively.

While, N-CQDs showed approximately 14 and 12 cm−1 blue-shifts for the D and G bands, respectively. The presence of N-atoms on the surface of N-CQDs increased the disorder owing to the presence of CN bands and amino groups on the surface of N-CQDs which leads to a higher ID/IG ratio in N-CQDs compared to CQDs [56]. In contrast to CQDs and N-CQDs, N, S-CQDs exhibit Raman peaks at 1368 and 1587 cm−1 for the D and G bands, respectively. This shift in the D and G bands can be ascribed to the structural distortion due to the bond distance of CC, CN, and CS [57]. Moreover, N, S-CQDs showed higher ID/IG (1.02) compared to N-CQDs (0.92) and CQDs (0.91), indicating the presence of a large number of defects in the form of edges and surface functional groups in N, S-CQDs [58,59]. The increase in the ID/IG ratio and the red-shift in the G band for N, S-CQDs confirms the doping of N and S atoms on the C core and these results corresponds well with XPS results.

Fig. 5 and Fig. S1a show the XPS results of N, S-CQDs and N-CQDs, respectively. As seen in the XPS survey spectra, N, S-CQDs are mainly composed of nitrogen, carbon, sulfur, and oxygen elements. The high-resolution C1s spectrum demonstrated the graphitic structure and presence of CN, CC, COC, CO, and CS bonds [60]. The O1s spectrum displays three peaks for CO (531.2 eV), COH (532.5 eV), and N-O (533.8 eV) bonds [61]. The N1s spectrum shows that nitrogen atoms are mainly present in pyridinic (399.6 eV) and pyrrolic (401.0 eV) forms. On the other hand, the high-resolution spectra of S2p band suggest the presence of C-S-C (163.1 (S2p3/2), and 164.4 eV (S2p1/2)), and SO (167.5 eV) units [62,63]. The XPS survey spectra of N-CQDs reveals that N-CQDs are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen (Fig. S1). The high-resolution N1s spectra reveal that the nitrogen atoms have a similar hybridization manner as N, S-CQDs.

The full valence band (VB) spectra were acquired by high-resolution-XPS for N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs (Fig. S2 and Fig. 6 a, respectively). As seen in Figures, the VB energy (EVB) onset appeared at 2.52 and 2.65 eV from the fermi level energy (Ef) for N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs, respectively. Therefore, using the band gap values obtained from UV-Vis (Fig. 3b; Tauc plots), the calculated energy gap between the fermi level and the CB energy (ECB) is 0. 92 and 0.66 eV for N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs, respectively. Hence the fermi level in N, S-CQDs is considerably closer to the CB, resulting in tailing structure as observed in the spectrum between the fermi level and CB onset [64]. The narrow gap between the fermi level and CB is expected because nitrogen and sulfur provide excess electrons leading to an upward shift of the fermi level [65]. These results indicate that co-doping of nitrogen and sulfur is most effective in reducing the band gap. These findings allowed to propose a full band energy diagram of prepared QDs. The band energy diagrams of N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs are shown in Fig. S2b and Fig. 6b, respectively. The determination of energy bands alignment of QDs is crucial step PV measurements. Most importantly, this application offers numerous advantages such as environmentally benign, cost-effective, “green” processes, and tailoring of band alignments and energies. From the CV (details are given in S2 and Fig. S3) and UV-Vis measurements, a full energy band diagram was drawn (Fig. 7 a). From Fig. 6b and Fig. 7a, we can demonstrate the basic contribution from VB, identify the potential charge trapping sites, and the key strength to these N, S-CQDs for constructing an optoelectronic device. The various low-energy orbitals in the VB gives the ideal approaches to tune the VBmax, providing a good base to fabricate an efficient optoelectronic device [66,67].

Fig. 7b shows the comparative current-density-voltage measurement results of Device-C, Device-N, and Device-NS, where CQDs, N-CQDs, and N, S-CQDs were used as light harvesters, respectively under 100 mW/cm2 light intensity, respectively. The full device structure contained a transparent conducting oxide fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO), a TiO2 hole blocking layer, CQDs as an active layer, a Pt counter electrode, and an I−/I electrolyte solution (Fig. 1b). As seen in Fig. 7b, the device with typical un-doped CQDs (Device-C) exhibited short-circuit current density (J sc) of 0.88 mA, open-circuit voltage (V oc) of 0.52 V, and a fill factor (FF) of 0.62, resulting in a PCE of 0.28 % under 100 mW cm−2 light intensity. On the other hand, the nitrogen-doped device (Device-N) gave Jsc 1.41 mA, Voc 0.61 V, FF 0.66, and the PCE of 0.57% under similar conditions. In contrast, Device-NS, showed a better performance with Jsc of 1.89 mA, Voc of 0.67 V, FF of 0.73, resulting in a PCE of 0.92% (Table 1 ). After irradiation, the excited electrons moved from VB to CB of CQDs, resulting in the separation of electron-hole pairs. The excited electrons rapidly moved to the TiO2 and transferred to the Pt counter electrode via an external circuit [38]. The N and S-dopant in the N, S-CQDs will be able to introduce additional energy levels between π and π* of carbon and oxygen atoms. Upon light irradiation, the excited N, S-CQDs can absorb the low-energy photons at a visible light range which leads to the formation of photoexcited electrons. Thus, more electrons are migrating to the CB of TiO2 resulting in a higher current density [34,68]. Fig. 7a presents the electronic transition between the N, S-CQDs and TiO2. Furthermore, the superior performance of the Device-NS could be ascribed to the favorable fermi level alignment N, S-CQDs CQDs (Fig. 6b), strong visible photoabsorption, and the charge separation and conduction in N, S-CQDs. The exceptional Jsc, Voc, and FF are basically dependent on the suitable band alliance with a negligible barrier between the CB of TiO2 and the N, S-CQDs which is crucial, resulting in effective migration of electrons towards the wide band gap semiconductor without compromising the Voc
[35]. The quick light response and stability of the devices were investigated; the measurement was performed with 10 second light on/off intervals. Under 100 mW/cm2 light illumination, the current increased rapidly, and exponentially decreased after light cut off up to 200 s (Fig. 7c). The incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) of all the devices is presented in Fig. 7d. As seen in the Fig. the IPCE spectra are fairly matching with the corresponding UV-Vis spectra of the QDs. Notably, The Device-NS showed highest IPCE values over 59% compared to the Device-C (23%) and Device-N (48%).

Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the prepared solar cell devices were also recorded to analyze the charge transfer behaviors of the devices. Fig. 8 shows the Nyquist plots of Device-C, Device-N, and Device-NS obtained at various biases. Z View software was used to fit the curves using a standard fitting model for QDSSCs. [5,69] The recombination resistance (Rrec) extracted from the corresponding Nyquist plots illustrated in (Fig. S4). The (Rrec) reflects the charge recombination process in TiO2/QD/electrolyte interfaces. As seen in (Fig. S4), the Device-NS exhibited higher Rrec compared to Device-C and Device-N, demonstrating the lower recombination rate than Device-C and Device-N, resulting in high density of electrons in the external circuit. This leads to the superior photovoltaic performance of the Device-NS among all other devices.

The photostability of solar cell is crucial for real-life applications [70]. Fig. 9 shows the PV performance of all the devices with respect to time recorded under 100 mW/cm−2 light intensity with no encapsulation in ambient conditions. The J-V scans were recorded after 0, 4,8, 16, and 48 h to find Jsc, Voc, FF, and PCE values. As seen in the Fig. Jsc of all the devices showed an increment during the first 4 h due to the improved dispersion of electrolyte solution through the photoanode, followed by a continuous decrease in the Jsc values. In contrast, Voc, FF, and PCE are constantly decreasing. The prolonged irradiation results in a reduced hole-recovery rate of the redox electrolyte [71]. The PCE value of Device-C and Device-N decreased much faster than Device-NS. Device-C and Device-N showed ∼34% and 29% reduction in PCE, while, Device-NS showed ∼19% reduction in efficiency after 48 h. The midgap states produced by the N, S-CQDs trapped the electrons and screen them from charge recombination with holes and the electrolyte. Hence, the reduced electron-hole recombination improved the photostability of Device-NS.

Mistry et al., [38] and Yang et al., [72] reported the performance of CQD-sensitized solar cells at low-intensity light and beyond sunny days. Mistry et al., [38] stated a maximum PCE of 1.20% for the N-CQDs sensitized solar cell device 10 mW/cm2 light intensity. Motivated by the above-mentioned results, we measured the performance of Device-NS at 100, 50, 25, and 10 mW/cm2 light intensities by applying corresponding filters. The key PV parameters of Device-NS are given in Table 2 . Fig. 10 shows the J-V curves of Device-NS under various light intensities. A continuing increase in the PCE (from 0.92 to 1.36 %) was observed upon decreasing the light intensity from 100 to 10 mW/cm2 . The lower photon to electron conversions could be the reason for light absorption and subsequent emissions at low intensity light measurements [38,53,73].

For all we know, this is the highest record among carbon based-quantum do sensitized solar cells (C-QDSCs). Table 3 shows a comparison of photovoltaic parameters achieved in this study and the already published literature of C-QDSCs. The N, S-CQDs were used solely as an absorbing layer without additional pre- or post-treatments. The Device-NS showed superior performance compared to Device-C and Device-N, as evidenced by VOC, FF, and PCE values. The device performance can further be improved by tailoring the absorbing layer's thickness and choosing a promising electron blocking layer.

In this study, we prepare nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots ((N, S-CQDS) using a single precursor, thioacetamide (TA) as carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur source. The N, S-CQDs were employed as a green sensitizer for quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSs). The co-doping of nitrogen and sulfur is a more efficient way to decrease the bandgap by decreasing the conduction band (CB) of QDs, which leads to the eased photocarriers injection in the TiO2 layer, resulting in improved photocurrent/photovoltaic performance of the solar cell device. The solar cell devices with undoped CQDs (Device-C) and N-doped-CQDs (Device-N) as a photoactive layer yielded a short-circuit current densities (Jsc) of 0.88 mA/cm2 and 1.41 mA/cm2 and a PCE of 0.28% and 0.57% at a light intensity of 100 mW/cm2, respectively. In contrast, the device with N, S-co-doped CQDs (Device-NS) as a photoactive layer provided Jsc of 1.89 mA/cm2 and a PCE of 0.92% under the same conditions. The superior performance of the Device-NS could be ascribed to the suitable band alignment of N, S-CQDs, which results in a little barrier between the CB of TiO2 and N, S-CQDs compared to N-CQDs and un-doped CQDs, which is the key and results in effective movement of electrons to the wide-bandgap semiconductor, leading to improved device performance. Further, the performance of Device-NS was also evaluated at a lower light intensity, yielding the maximum PCE of 1.36% reported so far. Moreover, Device-NS showed more stability over 48 h compared to Device-C and Device-N. These results suggest that N, S-CQDs could offer great opportunities for high-efficiency solar cell devices. Undeniably, the achieved PCE is not satisfactory; however, the upgraded device fabrication and structural design may improve PCE and current densities while sustaining the high open-circuit voltage.
Shahina Riaz: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Soo-Jin Park: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Technological Innovation R&D Program (S2848103) funded by the Small and Medium Business Administration (SMBA, Korea). This work was also supported by Nano-Convergence Foundation(www.nanotech2020.org) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT(MSIT, Korea)& the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy(MOTIE, Korea). [Project Name: Development of high-efficiency activated carbon filter for removing indoor harmful elements (VOCs, radon, bacteria, etc.)]

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2021.09.009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
